briefing.opening: Good morning. We will now begin the climate briefing for July 12, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Extreme Climate Phenomena Striking the World and Their Impacts
sections.content: Recently, 'once-in-a-century abnormal climate' phenomena have been occurring frequently worldwide, leading to warnings from climate scholars that even ordinary typhoons could result in major catastrophes [1]. Domestically, the 'super El Niño' is intensifying a disaster polarization phenomenon, bringing both torrential rains and severe droughts simultaneously [3]. Internationally, extreme weather events are also being reported, including heatwaves in Philadelphia [15], devastating floods in Wallonia, Belgium, and Lagos, Nigeria [20, 25], and an increased risk of hurricane floods in California [19]. This situation suggests that even specific regions like Brittany are not safe from climate change [16], and direct impacts of climate change, such as rising temperatures in the Vernon area [22], are being detected across the globe. This clearly demonstrates that the climate crisis is no longer a distant future concern but a serious, ongoing threat.

sections.title: ⚠️ Challenges of Energy Transition and Infrastructure Limitations
sections.content: Amidst the acceleration of the transition to renewable energy, the limitations of grid infrastructure are emerging as a major challenge. In India, bottlenecks in solar power due to aging grids threaten the achievement of the '2030 Renewable Energy Target' [11]. In the United States, although over 90GW of renewable energy is expected to be added by 2026, a surge in data center demand is pressuring grid reliability [13]. Notably, carbon emissions from data centers of big tech companies like Microsoft, Amazon, and Google increased by nearly a fifth over the past year, reaching approximately one-third of France's total emissions [24], indicating the severe impact of digital economy growth on energy demand and carbon emissions. Domestically, resolving the connection backlog for an additional 1GW of renewable energy by 2030 is identified as a key factor for energy transition [8], and civil society is urging a shift to renewable energy instead of new nuclear power plants, demanding the withdrawal of the Yeongdeok new nuclear power plant site selection [9].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Carbon Neutrality Practices and Green Transition Efforts
sections.content: Domestic and international efforts for carbon neutrality and green transition are unfolding in various ways to address the climate crisis. Domestically, the Wonju Climate Change Promotion Center is operating 'moss ball making' as a carbon-neutral experiential education program during summer to encourage citizen participation [2], and Daejeon Seo-gu donated 30.45 million won in carbon-neutral point contributions, setting an example of voluntary participation [5]. Furthermore, policy support is being strengthened, including the proposal of a law for priority support for green transition of SMEs [4] and Hwasun-gun securing national funding for eco-friendly projects [10]. Internationally, there are calls to support Mongolia's accelerated transition to renewable energy and high-efficiency heating systems [12], and China is leading the eco-friendly mobility transition by enhancing global competitiveness in electric vehicles, especially electric trucks [14]. Korea District Heating Corporation [7] and Daewon 2-dong, Osan City [6] are also participating in climate action through the implementation of carbon-neutral strategies and daily practices.

sections.title: 📰 Social Inequality and Perception Gaps in Climate Change
sections.content: Climate change is not merely an environmental issue but also exposes social inequality and perception gaps. According to one study, women are paying the price as the Earth gets hotter due to climate change [17], and retired women are most concerned about climate change, while working men are least concerned [23], demonstrating differences in perception and vulnerability to the climate crisis based on gender and social status. Within this social context, political trends attacking climate change behavioral science have also been reported [21], re-emphasizing the importance of climate action based on scientific consensus. Furthermore, a warning that climate change could lead to the extinction of the first bird species in the UK [18] highlights another serious threat of biodiversity loss, emphasizing that the climate crisis is an all-encompassing social issue.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severity of the global climate crisis, alongside various efforts and challenges to overcome it. This is a time that demands continuous attention and action. This concludes the climate briefing for July 12, 2026.

articles.original_title: ‘Every time the rain falls, the fear comes back’: life in Lagos under the constant threat of floods
articles.description: As Nigeria braces for another season of devastating rains, people affected describe the mental toll of repeatedly rebuilding their livesMurky water first tore down a perimeter fence, then bubbled into the yard before spilling into every room. Within minutes, electronics, kitchen appliances, furniture, documents and academic certificates lay submerged.With the water rising rapidly, Daniel Ebiesua evacuated his home in the Shogunle area of Lagos, with his wife, their two-week-old baby, four-year-o

summary.phenomenon: In the Shogunle area of Lagos, Nigeria, Daniel Ebiesua and his family evacuated their home due to rapid flooding, which submerged electronics, kitchen appliances, furniture, documents, and academic certificates. This occurred as Nigeria prepares for another season of devastating rains.
summary.cause: Murky water breached a perimeter fence, flowed into the yard, and inundated every room, occurring during a devastating rainy season.
summary.outlook: Affected individuals are experiencing the mental toll of repeatedly rebuilding their lives, and with Nigeria bracing for another devastating rainy season, the threat of continued flooding remains.
